question: Using the rules of inference, show that the premises 'If it is raining, then the ground is wet' (R → W) and 'It is raining' (R) lead to the conclusion 'The ground is wet' (W).
expectedPoints: Identify the argument form as Modus Ponens.
State the rule: From p → q and p, one can conclude q.
Apply the rule to the given premises: From R → W and R, conclude W.

question: Prove the validity of the argument: 'If I study, then I will pass the exam. If I pass the exam, then I will celebrate. Therefore, if I study, then I will celebrate.' Use rules of inference and clearly name each step.
expectedPoints: Let S represent 'I study', P represent 'I pass the exam', and C represent 'I will celebrate'.
The premises are S → P and P → C. The conclusion is S → C.
This is an application of the Hypothetical Syllogism rule: From p → q and q → r, one can conclude p → r.

question: Given the premises: (1) ¬p ∨ q, (2) ¬r → ¬q, and (3) p; use the rules of inference to prove the conclusion r. Provide a formal step-by-step proof, justifying each step with the name of the rule used.
expectedPoints: From premise (1) ¬p ∨ q and premise (3) p, apply Disjunctive Syllogism to conclude q. (Since p is true, ¬p is false, forcing q to be true).
From the conclusion q and premise (2) ¬r → ¬q, apply Modus Tollens. (Since q is true, ¬q is false. Therefore, the antecedent ¬r must also be false, meaning r is true).
The final conclusion is r, proven through the sequential application of Disjunctive Syllogism and Modus Tollens.
